request.question: Rock 'n' Roll Music was an American compilation of album tracks, including the 1965 album opener, with cover versions released by which iconic band?

title: Rock 'n' Roll Singer
I Hate Rock 'n' Roll
Just... Fabulous Rock 'n' Roll
Drive My Car
Rock 'n' Roll (John Lennon album)
Rock 'n' Roll Music (album)
World Rock'n'Roll Confederation
What's Next to the Moon (album)
It's Only Rock 'n Roll
Iowa Rock 'n' Roll Hall of Fame
sentences: Rock 'n' Roll Singer is the debut solo EP from Mark Kozelek.

The EP was released on June 13, 2000, and was released while Kozelek's final album with his previous band Red House Painters (the 1998 album "Old Ramon", which didn't get a release until 2001) was in limbo with record label mergers.

"Rock 'n' Roll Singer" contains three original tracks and four covers: "Rock 'n' Roll Singer," "You Ain't Got a Hold on Me," and "Bad Boy Boogie" originally by AC/DC, and "Around and Around" originally by John Denver.
"I Hate Rock 'n' Roll" is a song by the Scottish alternative rock group The Jesus and Mary Chain.
It was the first single from the band's compilation album, "Hate Rock 'n' Roll".
It was released together with 3 other tracks by Blanco y Negro Records in June 1995 and reached #61 in the UK single charts.
This release was the last release for the band on this label.
The 10" format was numbered and limited to 5000 copies.
The reworked version of the track, which was dubbed as "I Love Rock 'n' Roll" was also included on the group's last album "Munki", released in 1998.
Just... Fabulous Rock 'n' Roll is a studio album by Cliff Richard, released 11 November 2016.
The album continues the rock 'n' roll theme of his previous studio album "The Fabulous Rock 'n' Roll Songbook".
It comprises covers of 14 classic rock 'n' roll songs and one new song "It's Better to Dream".
It features Elvis Presley in duet with Richard in "Blue Suede Shoes" and Peter Frampton on guitar in "Dimples".
The album reached number 4 on the UK Albums Chart and has been certified Gold for sales over 100,000 in the UK.
"Drive My Car" is a song by the Beatles, written primarily by Paul McCartney, with lyrical contributions from John Lennon.
It was first released on the British version of the band's 1965 album "Rubber Soul"; it also appeared in North America on the "Yesterday and Today" collection.
The upbeat, lighthearted "Drive My Car" was used as the opening track for both albums.
Over the years the song has been covered by many artists such as former Spice Girls member Melanie C and Lulu, and the US band Breakfast Club.
which was featured in the 1988 film "License to Drive", that was played over the opening credits.
Rock 'n' Roll is the sixth studio album by John Lennon.
Released in 1975, it is an album of late 1950s and early 1960s songs as covered by Lennon.

Recording the album was problematic and spanned an entire year: Phil Spector produced sessions in October 1973 at A&M Studios, and Lennon produced sessions in October 1974 at Record Plant Studios (East).
Lennon was being sued by Morris Levy over copyright infringement of one line in his song "Come Together".
As part of an agreement, Lennon had to include three Levy-owned songs on "Rock 'n' Roll".
Spector disappeared with the session recordings and was subsequently involved in a motor accident, leaving the album's tracks unrecoverable until the beginning of the "Walls and Bridges" sessions.
With "Walls and Bridges" coming out first, featuring one Levy-owned song, Levy sued Lennon expecting to see Lennon's "Rock 'n' Roll" album.
Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles that consists of previously released Beatles tracks.
The double album was issued on 7 June 1976 in the United States, on Capitol Records (catalogue number SKBO 11537), and on Parlophone (PCSP 719) in the United Kingdom, four days later.

The album is a combination of some notable Lennon–McCartney originals, such as "Drive My Car", "Revolution", "Back in the U.S.S.R." and "Get Back", George Harrison's "Taxman", and a dozen cover versions of songs written by significant rock and roll composers of the 1950s, including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams.
"Rock 'n' Roll Music" was the first Beatles album to include "I'm Down", which had previously only been available as the B-side of the "Help!
" single.
World Rock 'n' Roll Confederation (WRRC) was registered in 1984, although its history traces to 1974.
It is an umbrella organization for national professional and amateur Rock and Roll dancesport federations.

Its statute that it "aims at promoting the physical training of its members by means of sporting activities in the form of Rock 'n' Roll dance tournaments, including the acrobatic variations (acrobatic rock 'n' roll) as well as Rock 'n' Roll and Boogie Woogie, Lindy Hop, Formation and alternative styles in line with the rules and sporting presentations".
The registered office is in Ljubljana, Slovenia.
What's Next to the Moon is a solo album by Mark Kozelek, released on January 10, 2001.
The album is composed entirely of drastically rearranged acoustic covers of Bon Scott-era AC/DC songs.
The album follows the release of his debut solo EP "Rock 'n' Roll Singer", which also features three AC/DC covers: "Rock 'n' Roll Singer," "You Ain't Got a Hold on Me," and "Bad Boy Boogie."
The versions of "Rock 'n' Roll Singer" and "Bad Boy Boogie" on this album are different from the versions on the EP.
It's Only Rock 'n Roll is the 12th British and 14th American studio album by The Rolling Stones, released in 1974.
It was the last Rolling Stones album for guitarist Mick Taylor and the songwriting and recording of the album's title track had a connection to Taylor's eventual replacement, Ronnie Wood.
The album also marked the 10th anniversary since the release of the band's debut album, "The Rolling Stones".
"It's Only Rock 'n Roll" has a firmer rock sound than the band's previous album, the more funk - and soul - inspired "Goats Head Soup".
"It's Only Rock 'n Roll" reached #1 in the US and #2 in the UK.
The Iowa Rock ‘n’ Roll Hall of Fame is a museum located in Arnolds Park, Iowa and maintained by the non-profit Iowa Rock ‘n’ Roll Music Association (IRRMA).
The mission of IRRMA is to “retain and honor the legacy of rock ’n’ roll music and preserve the history of music in the state of Iowa.”

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Rock 'n' Roll Music was an American compilation of album tracks, including the 1965 album opener, with cover versions released by which iconic band?
hop1_docs: The Pretty Things (album) | The Pretty Things is the self-titled 1965 release by the English rock band The Pretty Things, which features mostly R&B and rock and roll cover versions.  A re-issue released in 2000 on CD featured every track from both the American and British versions of the album.

I Hate Rock 'n' Roll | "I Hate Rock 'n' Roll" is a song by the Scottish alternative rock group The Jesus and Mary Chain.  It was the first single from the band's compilation album, "Hate Rock 'n' Roll".  It was released together with 3 other tracks by Blanco y Negro Records in June 1995 and reached #61 in the UK single charts.  This release was the last release for the band on this label.  The 10" format was numbered and limited to 5000 copies.  The reworked version of the track, which was dubbed as "I Love Rock 'n' Roll" was also included on the group's last album "Munki", released in 1998.

Rock 'n' Roll Music (album) | Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles that consists of previously released Beatles tracks.  The double album was issued on 7 June 1976 in the United States, on Capitol Records (catalogue number SKBO 11537), and on Parlophone (PCSP 719) in the United Kingdom, four days later.  The album is a combination of some notable Lennon–McCartney originals, such as "Drive My Car", "Revolution", "Back in the U.S.S.R." and "Get Back", George Harrison's "Taxman", and a dozen cover versions of songs written by significant rock and roll composers of the 1950s, including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams.  "Rock 'n' Roll Music" was the first Beatles album to include "I'm Down", which had previously only been available as the B-side of the "Help! " single.

Banquet (album) | Banquet is the fourth album by German progressive rock band Lucifer's Friend, released in 1974.  By this time there was no hard rock sound, it was now strictly progressive rock mixed with jazz fusion.  The song "Our World Is a Rock 'n' Roll Band" was the opening track on some versions (i.e. the U.S. version) of the original LP release, but the only CD releases to date do not include this track, which can instead be found as a bonus track on the Repertoire Records CD release of their self-titled debut.

Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets | Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets is a compilation album by American rock singer–songwriter Bob Seger.  The double-disc album was released on November 21, 2011 and contains 26 remastered tracks from throughout Seger's career, which spans more than four decades.  Included are the original mono version of "Ramblin' Gamblin' Man", Seger's first hit with The Bob Seger System from 1968, the classic Christmas song "The Little Drummer Boy" from 1987's "A Very Special Christmas", which makes its first appearance on a Seger album, and previously unreleased cover versions of Tom Waits' "Downtown Train" and Little Richard's "Hey, Hey, Hey, Hey (Going Back to Birmingham)."  There is also a Walmart exclusive edition that includes the bonus track "Living Inside My Heart," a song from the soundtrack of the 1986 film "About Last Night...", which has also never before been released on any Bob Seger album.  Two songs on this compilation album are edited compared to the original releases: "We've Got Tonight" is the single edit, which is about one minute shorter than the album version, and "Katmandu" is a newly edited version which omits the second verse, making the song also about one minute shorter compared to the original album version.  In the US it was certified gold and platinum in June 2013 by the RIAA.

His Best (Bo Diddley album) | His Best is a 1997 greatest hits compilation album by American rock and roll icon Bo Diddley released by Chess and MCA Records on April 8, 1997 (see 1997 in music).  The album was re-released by Geffen Records on April 17, 2007 as The Definitive Collection with a different album cover.  "The Definitive Collection" reached #2 on "Billboard" magazine's Blues Albums chart on June 21, 2008, which was the week that the album debuted on the charts.

Icon (Blink-182 album) | Icon is a compilation album from the American rock band Blink-182, released on March 29, 2013.  "Icon" was released following the band's departure from Interscope Records in 2012 and compiles the band's biggest singles, covering material from "Dude Ranch" (1997) to "Blink-182" (2003).
hop1_titles: Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets
response.summary_1: Passage evidence: "'Rock 'n' Roll Music' is a compilation album by The Beatles" and the U.S. release date was June 7, 1976; the compilation "consists of previously released Beatles tracks" and includes "a dozen cover versions of songs written by significant rock and roll composers of the 1950s, including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams." Missing/next-hop: identify which specific "1965 album opener" track appears on the Rock 'n' Roll Music tracklist and then find which iconic band released cover versions of that specific 1965 opener. Use the compilation's track listing and the named 1965 opener title as retrieval anchors.
response.hop2_query: Rock 'n' Roll Music Beatles US 1976 compilation track listing 1965 album opener cover versions band
hop2_docs: Rock 'n' Roll Music (album) | Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles that consists of previously released Beatles tracks.  The double album was issued on 7 June 1976 in the United States, on Capitol Records (catalogue number SKBO 11537), and on Parlophone (PCSP 719) in the United Kingdom, four days later.  The album is a combination of some notable Lennon–McCartney originals, such as "Drive My Car", "Revolution", "Back in the U.S.S.R." and "Get Back", George Harrison's "Taxman", and a dozen cover versions of songs written by significant rock and roll composers of the 1950s, including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams.  "Rock 'n' Roll Music" was the first Beatles album to include "I'm Down", which had previously only been available as the B-side of the "Help! " single.

Got to Get You into My Life | "Got to Get You into My Life" is a song by the Beatles, first released in 1966 on the album "Revolver".  It was written by Paul McCartney, though officially credited to Lennon–McCartney.  The song is a homage to the Motown Sound, with colourful brass instrumentation, and lyrics that suggest a psychedelic experience.  "It's actually an ode to pot," McCartney explained.  A cover version by Cliff Bennett and the Rebel Rousers peaked at number six in 1966 in the UK.  The song was issued in the United States as a single from the "Rock 'n' Roll Music" compilation album in 1976, six years after the Beatles disbanded.  It reached number seven on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart, the Beatles' last top ten US hit until their 1995 release "Free as a Bird".

(I Got No Kick Against) Modern Jazz | (I Got No Kick Against) Modern Jazz is a 1995 tribute album by various jazz artists and bands from the GRP Records label.  It consists of jazz cover versions of songs originally by The Beatles.  The album's title comes from the lyrics of the Beatles's cover of the Chuck Berry song, "Rock and Roll Music", which was originally released on the studio album "Beatles for Sale".

Hot Tracks (album) | Hot Tracks is a compilation album by the Scottish hard rock band Nazareth, released in 1976.  The album covers from 1973's "Razamanaz" album to 1976's "Play 'n' the Game".  The album also features a slightly longer version of "'This Flight Tonight" and the single version of 'I Want to Do Everything for You'.

Birthday (Beatles song) | "Birthday" is a song written by Lennon–McCartney and performed by the Beatles on their double album "The Beatles" (often known as "the White Album").  It is the opening track on the third side of the LP (or the second disc in CD versions of the record).  The song is an example of the Beatles' return to more traditional rock and roll form, although their music had increased in complexity and it had developed more of its own characteristic style by this point.  Surviving Beatles McCartney and Ringo Starr performed it for Starr's 70th birthday at Radio City Music Hall on 7 July 2010.

List of cover versions of Beatles songs | This is a list of cover versions by music artists who have recorded one or more songs written and originally recorded by English rock band The Beatles.  Many albums have been created in dedication to the group, including film soundtracks, such as "I Am Sam" (2001) and "Across the Universe" (2007) and commemorative albums such as "Sgt. Pepper Knew My Father" (1988) and "This Bird Has Flown" (2005).

The Pretty Things (album) | The Pretty Things is the self-titled 1965 release by the English rock band The Pretty Things, which features mostly R&B and rock and roll cover versions.  A re-issue released in 2000 on CD featured every track from both the American and British versions of the album.
hop2_titles: Got to Get You into My Life
(I Got No Kick Against) Modern Jazz
List of cover versions of Beatles songs
response.summary_2: The 1965 album opener included on Rock 'n' Roll Music is "Drive My Car" (passage 1). The supplied passages do not name which iconic band released cover versions of that song, so that detail remains unspecified.
